clustering_method: [hierarchical then k-means] hierarchical agglomerative clustering with Ward's minimum variance method and squared Euclidean distance, followed by k-means
validation_method: [2. separation on out-of-model features] multinomial logistic regression across demographic and sample characteristic features
contextualization: none
summary: Attempted to identify clusters of concussion could be identified based on health-related quality of life measures at 10 weeks post-injury. Found 3-cluster solution: (1) Full Recovered HRQoL, (2) Partially Recovered HRQoL, and (3) Partially Recovered with Poor Physical Health HRQoL. (1) had normal HRQoL. (2) had poor mental, social, energy, physical HRQoL. (3) had very poor physical HRQoL, poor mental, social, energy HRQoL. Interpret significance of subtypes as identifying fatigue/pain as potential at risk group for HRQoL recovery (though this is not entirely substantiated by the analytical design) and that fatigue and pain management may be beneficial intervention approaches for those with ongoing HRQoL difficulties post injury.

time_post_injury: less than 120 h, median 50.03 h, iqr 27.20 h to 70.47 h
validation_method: [5. generalization/held-out sample] classifier propagation to held out sample
contextualization: References that prior subtyping studies focus primarily on self-reported symptom batteries rather than physiology measures. Mentions this is the first study examining electrophysiological subtypes of concussion.
notes: Additional inclusion criteria include no electrode-preventing abnormalities AND not on psychoactive prescription medication AND no history of brain surgery or neurological disease AND not pregnant AND not intoxicated AND not experiencing active fever AND spoke English
summary: This study aimed to identify subtypes based on EEG profiles to identify brain activity-based subtypes. Did feature selection, clustering, and classifier training in a training set and propagated labels to a held out sample for validation purposes. Found 5 subtype solution. subtypes were generalizable and associated with return to activity - demonstrating potential clinically relevant subtypes from purely physiological data.

journal: the journal of head trauma rehabilitation
time_post_injury: less than 2 years
clustering_method: [hierarchical then k-means] hierarchical clustering to determine number of clusters followed by k-means
validation_method: [3. stability/robustness] replication by both k-means and hierarchical clustering
[2. separation on out-of-model features] check for significance across variety of external variables
contextualization: none
summary: Exploratory subtyping of military personnel sustaining combat-related concussion. Clustered based on symptoms and PTSD scores and found 4 subtypes: (1) high PTSD, (2) high cognitive/headache complaints, (3) mood, cognitive, and headache complaints, (4) low symptoms. The asserted significance is that these clusters may be suitable to use for different treatment assignment, though future work is stated as needed to validate this claim.

time_post_injury: daily from mean 6.63 to mean 41.63, sd 8.12
contextualization: qualitatively describe some similarities to rosenblatt et al., 2021.
notes: Intervention study using HeadOn web application.
summary: Subtyped patients based on symptoms and found a generally monotonic 3-cluster solution: (1) low symptoms, (2) medium symptoms, (3) high symptoms. The lowest symptom cluster was primarily male. The reported significance appears to primarily be related to improving support for patients with high symptom burden.

journal: ieee/acm transactions on computational biology and bioinformatics
time_post_injury: A pre-injury time point of unspecified time, once below 6 hours, from the 24-48 hour post-injury time point, once at asymptomatic, once at unrestricted rtp, once 7 days post-rtp, once 6 months post-rtp
validation_method: [1. internal validation (e.g. silhouette)] - Similarity matrix index
[1. internal validation (e.g. silhouette)] - Mean index accuracy
[2. separation on out-of-model features] - linear mixed models, Conover tests, and pairwise t-tests used to relate clusters to outcome measures and demographic and injury variables
contextualization: Reference some prior work but do not attempt to contrast their derived clusters with clusters from prior studies.
missing_data_handling: random forest with fully conditional specification single imputation
notes: Created 4 different cluster solutions. Acknowledged multiple imputation would be better but is challenging for cluster analysis. Concussion definition comes from CARE study which itself uses evidence-based concussion guidelines, citing Carney et al., 2014.
summary: Clustered concussion patients based on blood biomarker level trajectories across multiple time points. Solutions were based on (1) tau, (2) gfap, (3) nf-l, or (3) uch-l1. Constructed one solution per biomarker trajectory. Found that 2 of 11 derived clusters (coming from GFAP and NF-L trajectories) were associated with increased probability of LOC/PTA at injury time.

time_post_injury: between 3 and 78 months post-injury, median 12 months

summary: Attempted to find subtypes of patients with PPCS based on attention deficits. Found 3 clusters labeled (1) mild sustained attentional deficits, (2) selective and divided attentional deficits, and (3) general attentional deficit. Reported significance is that attention impairment subtypes of patients with PPCS exist.

journal: the journal of head trauma rehabilitation
time_post_injury: at least 3 months post-injury, range of 3 to 52 months post-injury, mean of 13.9 months and SD of 11.8 months
contextualization: References prior concussion subtyping literature and poses current study as focusing on chronic post-injury time point. Qualitatively compares clustering results to those derived in prior study as similar.
summary: This study aimed to identify subtypes of patients with PPCS based on those symptoms. Found a 4 cluster solution described as (1) minimal symptoms, (2) moderate to severe cognitive and affective symptoms, (3) moderate to severe cognitive and affective symptoms and moderate somatic and sensory complaints, and (4) global complaints. Report significance as evidence of the value of patient's subjective complaints, that distinct subtypes of symptoms existed

time_post_injury: 9.2 (7.9) years. Based on lifetime history of veterans so the range is wide.

validation_method: [2. separation on out-of-model features] Kruskal-Wallis and Chi-squared tests associating clusters to outcome measures.
contextualization: References common symptom-based subtype classification symptom. Specifically, discusses the existing 'vestibular' subtype as an overgeneralization. Specifically qualitatively relates derived vestibular-deficient subtype to existing vestibular subtype.
summary: Calculated subtypes of military service members and veterans based on binary features of having abnormal scores related to visual-, vestibular-, and proprioception-related balance problems. Identified 8 subtypes labeled (1) all intact, (2) visual deficient, (3) vestibular deficient, (4) proprioception deficient, (5) visual dominant, (6) vestibular dominant, (7) proprioception dominant, and (8) all deficient. Suggest significance of further stratifying typical vestibular subtype based on specific symptoms experienced.

time_post_injury: less than 6 hours for solutions 1 and 2, between 24 and 48 hours for solutions 3 and 4
contextualization: Qualitatively contextualizes with Kontos et al.
missing_data_handling: multiple imputation by MICE (separately replicated for all datasets)
summary: Generated 4 cluster solutions: (1) males < 6h, (2) females < 6h, (3) males 24 - 48 h, (4) females 24 - 48h based on post-concussion symptoms. Each solution had 2 clusters, where C1 was generally better than C2.

summary: Clustered general population using diffusion MRI, resting-state functional MRI, or both. Clustering by both resulted in two-cluster solution: (1) subtype A, which represented a mild PCS group, and (2) subtype B, which represented a severe PCS group.

time_post_injury: median of 177 days, range of 3-40 months

joint tmt b and wcst perseverative

contextualization: [Qualitatively contextualizes against subtypes from prior study] Technically contextualizes against similar subtypes derived by a former study, but this is achieved as a consequence of only manually clustering from a single categorical variable (mechanism of injury).
summary: The authors generate two solutions. The first is derived from subtyping according to mechanism of injury, resulting in (1) MVA, (2) fall, (3) assault, (4) fall-object, (5) sport, (6) MV-pedestrian. The second is derived from a summary mechanism-of-injury-category resulting in (1) HSO head struck object, (2) HSNO head struck no object, and (3) OSH object struck head. Found that outcomes were associated with mechanism of injury. Suggested significance of findings was a new way to classify subtypes of concussion and improve outcome predictions and service coordination.

time_post_injury: mean of 4 days, SD of 4 days
validation_method: [1. internal validation (e.g. silhouette)] Davies-Bouldin index was used to select k-means over graph and model-based clustering.
contextualization: [none]
notes: miRNA expression levels are based on visual assessment of a heatmap.
summary: This study aimed to subtype patients based on salivary miRNA levels. A 10 cluster solution (labeled clusters 1 to 10) was found which was capable of distinguishing patients apart on multiple factors including some post-concussion symptoms. The reported significance includes subtyping across an exclusively biological domain, which may be particularly relevant for stratifying patients for clinical trials, and deriving concussion clusters that may differ by their underlying pathophysiology.

time_post_injury: 61.6 (51.8) months, min of 0.3 months, max of 360 months

contextualization: References prior subtyping literature, qualitatively contrasts overall solution against prior overall solutions.
summary: The aim of this study was to identify subtypes of concussion in active-duty personnel based on personality-related questions from the Personality Assessment Inventory. The study found a two-cluster solution labeled as (1) low symptoms and (2) high symptoms groups. The authors consider this solution to be representative of simply the broad distribution of general severity rather than necessary evidence supporting distinct subtypes. Intriguingly, part of the reported significance is to note that active-duty personnel may not have distinct class structures, and that the single factor of overall severity may be most helpful to address in the clinical setting.

validation_method: [5. generalization/held-out sample] Model extension to held-out validation set.
contextualization: References prior subtyping literature and qualitatively compares derived subtypes to those in a prior study. The prior study was conducted by the same research group.
notes: Also tracks Hispanic ethnicity as a separate binary variable.
summary: In this study, the authors aimed to identify subtypes of veterans with concussion based on neurobehavioural symptoms. Seven clusters were found, labeled based on the sorts of symptoms they experienced: (1) global, (2) headache-cognitive-mood, (3) headache-mood, (4) cognitive-mood, (5) headache-sleep, (6) anxiety, and (7) minimal. The reported significance of the study included finding an association between outcome and race, ethnicity, and sex. Specifically, that female, Black, and Hispanic veterans were generally more likely to end up in high PCS endorsement categories.

time_post_injury: 28 days post-injury
contextualization: Qualitatively compares derived clusters to those reported in existing literature.
summary: The aim of this study was to identify patient subtypes of pediatric concussion based on symptoms from the post-concussion symptom inventory. 5 patient subtypes were identified, labeled (1) resolved/minimal symptomatic, (2) mild, (3) moderate, (4) severe, and (5) profound. The reported significance of the study includes better informing clinicians of how symptoms can co-occur. Only a qualitative description of these clusters is provided. The mildly symptomatic group had higher severity in fatigue and cognitive emotional domains. The moderately symptomatic group had higher severity in balance/dizziness/headaches or in headache/cognitive/fatigue. The highly symptomatic group is most highly symptomatic in cognitive/emotional and fatigue domains and least symptomatic in physical symptoms. The profoundly symptomatic group is highly symptomatic in all domains except for a subset for vestibular-ocular symptoms.

time_post_injury: 24 months
clustering_method: [hierarchical] hierarchical cluster analysis using Ward’s minimum variance method
contextualization: [none]
notes: concussion definition based on ICD-9-CM codes: 850.0, 850.11, 850.5, or 850.9. Cases with other TBI codes were excluded.
summary: The study aimed to define subtypes of concussion based on a variety of symptoms 2 years after their injuries. 5 subtypes were identified: (1) no morbidity, (2) back pain, (3) tinnitus/memory loss, (4) posttraumatic stress disorder/postconcussion syndrome, and (5) multimorbidity. The reported significance of their subtypes included revealing different subtype distributions for men and women. Specifically, that women had higher membership in the back pain and multimorbidity profiles and lower membership in the tinnitus/memory loss profile.

journal: the journal of head trauma rehabilitation
time_post_injury: Within 2-weeks post-injury

clustering_features.feature: WOCQ planful problem solving subscale mean score

validation_method: [4. stability/robustness - alternate clustering method] Checked that hierarchical clustering solution sufficiently agreed with k-means clustering result.
contextualization: Qualitatively relates their clustering results to those reported in previous study (Moore et al., 1989).
summary: This study aimed to identify patient subtypes of concussion based on post-injury coping strategies. The main types of coping strategies included problem-focused (actively addressing problems at hand) and avoidant (avoid or escape the problem at hand). Three coping profiles were identified: (1) high problem-focused/high avoidant, (2) high problem-focused/low avoidant, and (3) low problem-focused/low avoidant. The significance of these subtypes included explaining a large proportion of the variance around depression, anxiety, and mental health quality of life measures at 3 months post-injury that were not explained by demographics and concussion severity. This is particularly important as coping strategies are modifiable factors.

validation_method: [4. stability/robustness - alternate clustering method] Confirmed cluster solutions through k-means and discriminant function analysis
contextualization: Qualitatively relates results to those from prior concussion clustering studies (e.g., Rabinowitz and Arnett, 2013)
notes: acknowledged race/ethnicity missing as a limitation. Significance stars in Table 3 appear to conflict with the written descriptions in Figure 5. Concussion definition required at least two postconcussion ImPACT assessments from a sports-related head injury
summary: This study aimed to identify subtypes based on verbal memory, visual reaction time, visual memory, and working memory either 72 hours, 4 - 7 days, or 8 - 30 days post injury. At the 72 hour and 4-7 day time points, 3 cluster solutions were labeled (1) average, (2) below average, and (3) mildly-to-moderately impaired. At the 8-30 day time point, the 2 cluster solution was labeled (1) average and (2) mildly impaired. They found that their derived clusters were associated with complicated recovery status, return-to-play time, and cognitive performance. They report key significance of the study as a tool to improve our ability to predict recovery outcomes and identify athletes at risk of complicated recovery.

time_post_injury: not reported
contextualization: Does not explicitly contextualize derived concussion subtypes against previously derived concussion subtypes.
summary: This study attempted to find subtypes based on comorbodities of pain, PTSD, and depression. Five subtypes were found: (1) moderately healthy, (2) mental health, (3) moderately healthy + decline, (4) polytrauma, and (5) polytrauma + improvement. The reported significance of these subtypes is that by better understanding the risk factors distinguishing (4) polytrauma from (5) polytrauma + improvement and between (1) moderately healthy and (2) moderately healthy + decline, we will be able to mitigate risk and improve patient outcomes.

summary: This study aimed to find subtypes based on variability scores derived from a wide range of neurocogntive and neuropsychological test measures at baseline and post-concussion time points. Two subtypes were found, labeled (1) low-variability and (2) high-variability. Membership in the high-variability (i.e., high intra-individual variability across variety of measures at either time point) group was associated with greater cognitive dysfunction following injury. The reported significance included identifying intraindividual variability as containing clinically meaningful information.

summary: This study aimed to find subtypes based on trajectories of fatigue, anxiety and depression, PTSD, and coping style. Four subtypes were found, labeled (1) complete recovery from fatigue in combination with active coping, (2) decreasing fatigue and decreasing passive coping, (3), persistent fatigue without emotional distress, and (4) persistent fatigue with emotional distress. The studies reported significance includes revealing that patients at risk of developing chronic fatigue issues can be identified at an acute time point based on subtype membership.

summary: This study attempted to identify subtypes based on the number of academic problems the child was experiencing. A two cluster solution was identified, labeled (1) high and (2) low in reference to the levels of self-reported academic problems. The reported significance of the study included identifying that these subtypes were associated with levels of symptom severity and executive dysfunction and informing clinicians about these features being useful for predicting perceived academic problems.

summary: This study aimed to identify subtypes based on post-concussion symptom factors. Five subtypes were found, labeled (1) minimally complex, (2) mildly complex, (3) moderately complex, (4) highly complex, and (5) extremely complex. The reported significance of this study included offering clinicians insight into concussion heterogeneity in terms of symptom complexity, which could subsequently inform clinical decision making.

contextualization: none
summary: This study aimed to identify subtypes based on demographic and clinical presentation variables. Five subtypes were identified, labeled clusters A to E. Clusters A contained a high proportion of patients with CT abnormalities and alcohol use. Cluster E contained a high proportion of patients with CT abnormalities. Clusters B, C, and D had nearly no CT abnormalities and were distinguished by prior neurological disease (low in cluster C, high in B and D) and abnormal systolic blood pressure (higher in B than D). Several other features distinguished these clusters. The reported significance of the study included that the subtypes distinguished patients across post-concussion outcomes at 90 and 180 days following injury and that these subtypes may be informative for building predictive models of TBI outcomes.

summary: This study identified 7 subtypes based on demographic and clinical presentation variables. The authors found two sets of subtype solutions by doing derivation in each of two datasets and validation in the other. The significance of the study was that the identified subtypes were generalizable and were separated across patient outcomes at 90 and 180 days post injury, indicating their utility for post-concussion prognosis.

journal: the american journal of sports medicine

contextualization: Referenced SRC symptom subtypes, but no prior patient subtypes of concussion.

notes: Included ethnicity (Hispanic identity) as a separate variable. LPA identified 6 profiles but two were collapsed due to small sample size (severe global impairment + moderate global impairment -> global impairment).
summary: This study aimed to identify subtypes based on acute (0-12 hour) post-concussive symptoms. Five subtypes were identified, labeled (1) low impairment, (2) high cognitive impairment, (3) high balance impairment, (4) high symptom severity, and (5) global impairment. These subtypes were associated with outcomes at 12 to 72 hours post-injury and distinguished patients across recovery times. The reported significance of the study was that the derived subtypes may aid in post-concussion outcome prognosis.

summary: This study aimed to identify subtypes based on illness percetion. Three subtypes were identified, labeled (1) high-adapters, (2) medium-adapters, (3) low-adapters. The reported significance of the study included that subtypes differed in terms of post-concussion outcomes over time and that the derived subtypes could be used to better inform clinicians about which patients are at greater risk for slow or incomplete recovery.

validation_method: [3. stability/robustness - perturbing sample] leave-one-out resampling check over 100 replications.
[4. stability/robustness - alternate clustering method] SOM replications are non-deterministic, and stability was examined over 100 replications. Also examined leave one feature out resampling.
contextualization: Relates identified clusters to previously identified symptom-based clusters.
notes: entry for mean age is actually median age.
summary: This study aimed to find subtypes based on a variety of vestibular and balance related measures. A two cluster solution was found, labeled (1) group-1 and (2) group-2. Group-1 had prominent vestibular disorders while group-2 was generally healthy. The reported significance of this study was that the presented approach could identify subtypes by objective measures rather than subjective symptoms.

time_post_injury: 0-48 hours

clustering_features.feature: previous concussion with symptoms at least 1 week

clustering_method: [finite mixture model] latent class analysis with parameter estimation by MLE treating group parameter k as an unknown.
contextualization: none
notes: orderings based on visual assessment of feature comparison figures. general pcs extraction based on supplementary tables 6 to 8.
summary: Derived three different cluster solutions by LCA from one of the domains of pre-morbid history, clinical presentation, or acute symptoms which revealed differences in risk of sustaining PPCS. Solution 1: (1) PH1 low probability of pre-injury risk factors, (2) PH2 high probability of prior concussion and symptoms lasting over a week, (3) PH3 high probability of anxiety / migraine / learning problems, (4) PH4 high probability of prior concussion and persistent symptoms. Solution 2: (1) CP1 low probability of clinical presentation findings, (2) CP2 moderate/high prob of mental status impairment, (3) CP3 moderate/high mental status impairment and elevated deficitis on cognition/balance, (4) CP4 high probability of deficitThen a broader cluster solution based on membership in each of those three solutions. Solution 3: 7 cluster solution ranging from AS1 to AS7 varying on acute symptom severity. Notably clinical presentation variables showed relatively little importance in PPCS prediction. The study asserts that the phenotypes shown can be useful for clinical management through improved understanding of risks of PPCS beyond clinician ability, but that replication is required.
